judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports independently reach the same correct diagnosis and hit all four expectations equally well; Report 1 is marginally stronger on (d) by explicitly proposing a shadow/parallel evaluation as the isolated verification method, while Report 2 only argues the model-swap can't test the root cause.

report_1.evidence: The file to fix is `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml`. It still says `expected_label_field: label` (config/scoring_config.yaml:2), but every record in `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` now stores its gold answer under the key `gold_label`.

report_2.evidence: The file to change is `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml`. It still says `expected_label_field: label`, but the eval set (`fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`) was migrated so every record's gold key is now `gold_label`.

report_1.evidence: The regression began on 2026-06-17, introduced by commit `53551e8` "chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)", authored 2026-06-17 13:00 UTC... `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` shows 0.91 on 2026-06-16 and 0.00 starting 2026-06-17, the exact day the commit landed

report_2.evidence: It began with commit `53551e8` — "chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)", authored 2026-06-17 13:00 UTC... `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` shows accuracy of 0.91 on 2026-06-16 and 0.00 starting exactly 2026-06-17

report_1.evidence: The earlier investigation never exercised the failing code path. It re-ran tickets through `pipeline/score_one.py`, which calls `predict_category` and prints the prediction — it never loads the config, never reads the eval set, and never compares against a gold label... The correct reproduction is trivial and deterministic: run the actual nightly entry point, `python3 -m pipeline.score_batch`, from the repo root

report_2.evidence: they re-ran failing tickets through `pipeline/score_one.py`, which only prints the predicted category and never compares it to a gold label, so it structurally cannot exhibit a scoring bug... The correct reproduction is to run the same entry point the nightly job runs — the batch scorer — not the single-item spot-check

report_1.evidence: Flipping a model/config on the shared nightly runner just to observe the effect is exactly the experiment-in-production pattern to avoid — if a comparison like this is ever genuinely needed, run the candidate in a shadow/parallel evaluation rather than changing the production nightly job.

report_2.evidence: even if a real model were involved, the failure is in the gold-label lookup, which is independent of whatever produces predictions: any model swap would still score 0.0 because every gold value resolves to `None`.
